title: Master Theorem and Sorting

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-07-algorithms-numerical-methods.md

setup.narrative: Recurrence T(n) = a * T(n/b) + f(n). Master Theorem says: compare f(n) to n^(log_b a). If f is asymptotically smaller (case 1), T = Theta(n^(log_b a)). If equal up to a polylog factor (case 2), T = Theta(n^(log_b a) * log^(k+1) n). If f is asymptotically larger and a regularity condition holds (case 3), T = Theta(f(n)).
rules: Merge sort: T(n) = 2 T(n/2) + n. a=b=2, log_b a = 1, f(n) = n = Theta(n^1). Case 2 with k=0 gives Theta(n log n).
Binary search: T(n) = T(n/2) + 1. a=1, b=2, log_b a = 0, f(n) = 1 = Theta(n^0). Case 2 with k=0 gives Theta(log n).
Quicksort worst case: bad pivot gives T(n) = T(n-1) + n -> Theta(n^2).
setup.question: What does the Master Theorem give for merge sort and binary search? What's the worst case for quicksort?

learningObjectives: Identify a, b, f(n) in a divide-and-conquer recurrence.
Distinguish quicksort's average vs worst case.

stages.label: Merge sort recurrence
stages.prompt: Merge sort recurrence: T(n) = 2 T(n/2) + n. Identify (a, b) and apply the Master Theorem.

options.text: a=2, b=2. log_b a = 1. f(n) = n = Theta(n^1). Case 2 with k=0 -> T(n) = Theta(n log n).

stages.explanation: Two recursive calls of size n/2 plus a Theta(n) merge step. log_b a = log_2 2 = 1; f(n) = Theta(n^1) -> Case 2 yields T(n) = Theta(n log n).

stages.label: Quicksort average vs worst
stages.prompt: Quicksort: average comparisons = sum_{p<q} 2/(q-p+1) = Theta(n log n). What gives the WORST case Theta(n^2)?

options.text: Already-sorted input with first-element pivot: each partition splits into sizes 0 and n-1 -> T(n) = T(n-1) + n -> Theta(n^2).
Quicksort is always Theta(n log n) -- there's no Theta(n^2) case.

stages.explanation: Bad pivot choice (first element on sorted input) gives unbalanced partitions. T(n) = T(n-1) + Theta(n) -> Theta(n^2). Randomized pivot or median-of-three avoids this in practice.

stages.label: Commit
stages.prompt: Match each algorithm to its complexity:
stages.template: Insertion sort worst-case: __[a]__.  Merge sort worst-case: __[b]__.  Quicksort average: __[c]__.  Binary search: __[d]__.
stages.explanation: Insertion: n^2. Merge: n log n. Quick (avg): n log n; Quick (worst): n^2. Binary search: log n. Sweep these from memory before any algorithm interview.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Why is quicksort often FASTER than merge sort in practice despite identical asymptotic average complexity? (Smaller constants, better cache locality, in-place.)
When IS insertion sort the right choice? (Small arrays / nearly sorted data; STL sort uses it as a base case.)
Compute T(n) = 4 T(n/2) + n^2 via Master Theorem. (a=4, b=2, log_b a = 2, f(n)=Theta(n^2) -> Case 2 -> Theta(n^2 log n).)
